text: The surgeon begins the intracorporeal esophageal jejunal stoma by clamping the distal esophagus using a laparoscopic intestinal clamp, preparing the site for further surgical manipulation.
The surgeon inserts a laparoscopic purse string clamp, known as a lap jack, through the twelve millimeter left middle trocar, preparing for the intracorporeal esophageal jejunal stoma procedure.
The surgeon positions the clamp as low as possible above the laparoscopic intestinal clamp on the distal esophagus, ensuring oncological safety.

The surgeon inserts one end of a two-headed straight needle into the upper hole of the lap jack and the other end into the lower hole, forming a purse string suture around the esophageal jejunal stoma.
The surgeon extracts the end of the suture through the right upper trocar, completing the placement of the purse string suture.
The surgeon transects the esophagus just below the lap jack using a hook or spatula-type Bovie, visibly separating the esophagus from surrounding tissue.
The surgeon releases the lap jack, allowing for the suctioning of any residual content from inside the esophagus.
The operator firmly grasps the esophageal wall while the first assistant holds the opposite end, maintaining tension on the tissue.
The surgeon inserts the anvil of a circular stapler, approximately twenty-five millimeters in size, into the open end of the esophagus.
The surgeon ties the previously placed purse string suture on the esophagus using Ruder's method, securing the suture around the esophageal tissue.
The surgeon opens the circular stapler inside the abdominal cavity and inserts it into the anvil positioned within the esophagus.
The surgeon cuts the nylon tape that is securing the jejunum to the circular stapler.
The surgeon fires the circular stapler, completing the anastomosis between the esophagus and jejunum.
The surgeon carefully opens and withdraws the circular stapler from the anastomosis site, ensuring the integrity of the newly formed connection between the esophagus and jejunum.
The surgeon uses a 60mm linear stapler cartridge to resect the entry hole, ensuring closure of the opening created during the procedure.
